meta.title: 735i Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: BMW 735i engines — M30, M62, N62. Real UK costs, cooling system & valve seal insight, and honest reliability data across four generations. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 735i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 735i engine costs £2,500–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. E23/E32 M30 engines start around £2,500; E38 M62 units start around £3,500; E65/E66 N62 units start around £4,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500. Main dealer new engines are no longer available. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends entirely on which generation you have. The E23 and E32 M30 straight-six is widely considered bulletproof — one of BMW's most durable engines ever. The E38 M62 V8 is smooth and refined but has cooling system vulnerabilities. The E65/E66 N62 V8 is the most complex, with valve stem seal and Valvetronic issues being common. The older the car, the simpler and more reliable the engine tends to be.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 735i?

acceptedAnswer.text: E23/E32 M30: Electronics, HVAC, fuel pumps — the engine itself is bulletproof. E38 M62: Cooling system failures (radiator, expansion tank, water pump), VANOS issues. E65/E66 N62: Valve stem seal failure (blue smoke on startup), Valvetronic issues (misfires), coolant pipe corrosion.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 735i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and which generation. Against a well-kept £8,000 E38 735i, a £5,000 recon is 63% — approaching our 70% BMW threshold. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold. For E23/E32 classics, values are rising — a £4,000 recon against a £10,000 car is 40% — worth it. For E65/E66, values are lower — a £6,500 recon against a £4,000 car is rarely justified.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 735i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. E23 (1977–1986): M30B35. E32 (1986–1992): M30B35. E38 (1996–2001): M62B35. E65/E66 (2001–2008): N62B36. The chassis code is the easiest tell.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the valve stem seals on the N62, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — valve stem seal replacement is a specialist repair costing £1,500–£3,500. It's a known issue on the N62 and is well-understood by BMW specialists. If you notice blue smoke on startup or after extended idling, have it inspected promptly. Ignoring it can lead to catalytic converter failure and, in extreme cases, engine damage from oil starvation.

eraMap.title: The 735i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The original 7 Series — a bulletproof M30 straight-six. Electronics and HVAC are the real weak points.
The most successful 735i — nearly 100,000 sold. Same bulletproof M30.
BMW's first V8 735i. Smooth and refined, but cooling systems and VANOS need vigilance.
The most complex 735i. Valvetronic, valve stem seals, and coolant pipes are the main concerns.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

rows.bestFor: No longer available from BMW — these engines are out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the varying costs across the 735i's four generations. M30 engines (E23/E32) are the cheapest to replace; N62 engines (E65/E66) are the most expensive due to complexity. Main dealer new engines are no longer available for any 735i generation.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 735i engine typically takes 12–20 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). V8 engines (E38/E65) require more labour than the straight-six (E23/E32).

cta.label: → Compare 735i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Cooling System Failures — M62 V8 (E38 735i)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature gauge fluctuations, sweet smell from the engine bay, radiator or expansion tank cracks.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (cooling system overhaul)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£6,500 (if overheating leads to head gasket or engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The M62 V8's cooling system is its Achilles heel. The plastic radiator, expansion tank, and water pump are known failure points. If you're experiencing coolant loss or overheating, have the entire cooling system inspected. Preventative replacement of the water pump, thermostat, and expansion tank is recommended at 60,000-mile intervals. This applies to the E38 735i only.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system failure guide

problems.title: Valve Stem Seal Failure — N62 V8 (E65/E66 735i)
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke from exhaust on startup, especially after idling for 5+ minutes, excessive oil consumption, smoke screen on acceleration.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 (valve stem seal replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£8,500 (if oil starvation leads to engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The N62's valve stem seals are a notorious failure point. If you notice blue smoke on startup or after extended idling, have the valve stem seals inspected immediately. Left unchecked, oil consumption can lead to catalytic converter failure and, in extreme cases, engine damage. This applies exclusively to the E65/E66 735i.

failureLink.label: Read the full valve stem seal failure guide

problems.title: Valvetronic / Misfire Issues — N62 V8 (E65/E66 735i)
problems.symptoms: Misfire under light throttle below ~2000 RPM, especially when cold; runs fine at higher RPMs; rough idle; engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (Valvetronic motor or sensor replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: Valvetronic issues are common on the N62. The system uses an electric motor to control valve lift, and these motors or their sensors can fail. If you experience misfires or rough running, have the Valvetronic system diagnosed. This applies exclusively to the E65/E66 735i.

failureLink.label: Read the full Valvetronic failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 735i? Check These First
buyIf: * E23/E32: A well-maintained M30 with documented service history — these are bulletproof
* E38: Cooling system overhaul completed (water pump, thermostat, radiator, expansion tank)
* E65/E66: Valve stem seals replaced and Valvetronic system inspected
* Any generation: Full service history with evidence of regular oil changes
avoidIf: * E38: Coolant loss or overheating with no documented cooling system work
* E65/E66: Blue smoke on startup (valve stem seals) or misfires (Valvetronic)
* Any generation: Poor service history — these are complex, high-mileage cars now
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 735i badge spans four decades of BMW flagship engineering — from the bulletproof M30 straight-six of the E23 and E32, through the refined M62 V8 of the E38, to the complex N62 V8 of the E65. The M30 cars are the most reliable; the N62 cars are the most demanding. Buy the generation that matches your budget and tolerance for complexity — but buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained 735i is a £5,000–£15,000 classic; a neglected one is a £5,000+ repair bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 735i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 735i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling, misfires, or blue smoke (N62: valve stem seals; M62: timing chain)
* Service History — including which engine era (M30/M62/N62) and confirmed oil-change intervals
* Coolant Check (E38) — Any history of coolant loss or cooling system work? (M62's Achilles heel)
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke on startup indicates valve stem seal failure (N62)
* Valvetronic Operation (E65) — Any hesitation, rough idle, or misfires? (Valvetronic warning)
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (N62 valve stem seals)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved VANOS, Valvetronic, or cooling system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.

items.label: * M30B35 (E23/E32)
items.value: Displacement: 3,430 cc (later 3,453 cc) · Cylinders: Straight-6, SOHC, 12 valves · Fuel: Bosch Motronic injection

items.label: * M62B35 (E38)
items.value: Displacement: 3,498 cc · Cylinders: V8, DOHC, 32 valves · Fuel: Direct injection

items.label: * N62B36 (E65/E66)
items.value: Displacement: 3,600 cc · Cylinders: V8, DOHC, 32 valves · Fuel: Direct injection · Valvetronic
